text:
Admission Date:  1976-01-15              Discharge Date:   2011-09-08

Date of Birth:  1991-04-24             Sex:   M

Service: SURGERY

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Joseph
Chief Complaint:
s/p fall

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None

History of Present Illness:
71 y/o male who fell day prior to presentation while ice
skating.  Pt. went home after fall and experienced unilateral
vision loss? that night.  Day of presentation pt. got up in a.m.
and went to get out of a chair and fell from standing.  When EMS
arrived, pt. combatative and ? of seizure like activity per
wife.  Pt. vomited at OSH and was intubated for protection.  Pt.
found to be in afib with no prior history.  CT at OSH showed a
chronic subdural hemorrhage on right frontal and acute component
on R. occipital with SAH.  Pt. medflighted to Seaside Behavioral Center for further
care.

Past Medical History:
HTN
Hypercholesterolemia


Social History:
Lives with wife, very active.  Pt. has a mixed drink daily.

Family History:
Non-contributory

Physical Exam:
101.5 63 18 117/67 96% intubated
Gen: pt. intubated and sedated
HEENT: laceration on back of right ear, no otorrhea or
rhinorrhea b/l, slcera anicteric
CV: rrr, nl s1/s2, no gross abnormalities
Lung: CTAB
Abd: soft, ND, Rectal - guiac negative
FAST: negative
Pelvis: stable
Ext: no gross deformities

Pertinent Results:
AP CHEST: There is an endotracheal tube whose tip is at the
level of aorta. The nasogastric tube tip is in the lower
esophagus and should be advanced for further placement. There
are patchy opacities in the right upper and right mid lung
zones. These may be secondary to contusions. No displaced rib
fractures are identified.

AP pelvis. There are no signs of acute fractures or
dislocations. Contrast material is seen within the bladder.

CXR (04-16):
1. Central venous catheter in satisfactory position, with no
pneumothorax.
2. Proximal location of nasogastric tube and endotracheal tube
cuff overinflation. Findings communicated by telephone to Dr.
Norris on the date of the study.

CT torso: Patchy right upper and lower lobe opacities and small
opacities in the left lower lobe. These findings are more likely
to be secondary to aspiration than pulmonary contusions.
2. Redemonstration of nondisplaced left C7 facet fracture. No
other fractures identified.
3. No intra-abdominal injury identified.
4. Aneurysmal dilatation of ascending and descending aorta.
5. NGT tube tip in esophagus.

CT C-spine: Left C7 articular pillar fracture, which is
nondisplaced, but extending to the superior articular surface,
this fracture contributes to the slight narrowing of the left
neural foramen at this level.

CT Head: Right-sided subdural hematoma, extending from the
frontal to the occipital portions, with some hematocrit effect.
While sulci appear decreased, basal cisterns and lateral
ventricles are still well seen.
2. Equivocal tiny left subdural hematoma.

MR Richard Elliott: No evidence of epidural hematoma. No abnormal
signal to suggest the presence of ligamentous injury.

CT Head (02-08): Evolving left subdural hematoma, largely
unchanged in size since 1976-01-15. 3-mm hypodense subdural
hygroma, not seen in the prior head CT.

MRA Neck Vessels: FINDINGS: There is no evidence of significant
internal carotid artery stenosis. The vertebral arteries are
patent. The portion of the left vertebral artery origin obscured
by venous contrast on the patient's outside head CTA appears to
be unremarkable on this examination.
IMPRESSION: No definite evidence of stenosis. No definite
evidence of arterial dissection.

Shoulder: No definite evidence of fracture, however, a small
ossific density at the region of the acromioclavicular joint may
represent a sequela of old injury or part of osteoarthritic
changes.

EKG (04-16): Atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular
response. Modest non-specific ST-T wave changes. No previous
tracing available for comparison


Brief Hospital Course:
Pt. transferred from OSH for sudural hematoma s/p multiple
falls.

Neuro: Neurosurgery was consulted for evaluation of SDH.  Pt.
was loaded with dilantin in the trauma bay.  There was a small
SDH which was unchanged on a follow up CT the day after
presentation.  Pt. was started on dilantin for a total of a
week.  Pt. will follow up in 4 weeks from injury with
neurosurgery with a CT scan prior to appointment.

Vasc: The OSH did a CTA which was reviewed by our
neuroradiologist and felt that the vertebral artery could not be
seen along the entire course.  Because of this vascular surgery
was consulted.  An MRA was performed that showed normal
vasculature.

Cards: Given pt's head bleed, tight control on his blood
pressure was maintained during his hospitalization

Resp: pt. intubated upon arrival.  Pt. weanted from vent. on HD
2 without difficulty.

MSK:  Pt. w/ c7 facet fx on CT so ortho/spine was consulted. Pt.
also had an MRI of C spine to better visualize this fracture.

GI: Pt. NPO and then advanced to a regular diet without a
problem.  Seth Rodriguez head injury, pt. was placed on protonix.

GU: Foley in place with good urine output.

.

Proph:  Pt. given pneumoboots, heparin SC and protonix.


Medications on Admission:
Atenolol 25'
Lipitor 20'
ASA 81'


Discharge Medications:
1. Oxycodone-Acetaminophen 5-325 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO
Q4-6H (every 4 to 6 hours) as needed for L shoulder pain.
Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
2. Pantoprazole 40 mg Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) Sig: One
(1) Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) PO Q24H (every 24 hours).
Disp:*30 Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.)(s)* Refills:*2*
3. Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID
(2 times a day).
Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
4. Phenytoin Sodium Extended 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule
PO TID (3 times a day) for 4 days.
Disp:*12 Capsule(s)* Refills:*0*


Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service

Facility:
VNA CARE MUTUAL

Discharge Diagnosis:
Subdural hygroma
Right posterior subdural bleed


Discharge Condition:
Good


Discharge Instructions:
You need to take all your medications as instructed.

You will need to wear that collar for 9 weeks. Do not lift
anything heavier than a gallon of milk.  You cannot drive during
this time and do not do vigorous activity.

Call the doctor or go to the ER if you experience new numbness,
weakness, tingling, bowel or bladder incontinence, nausea,
vomiting, fevers, chills or any other changes in your medical
condition that concern you.

Followup Instructions:
You need to follow up in trauma clinic in 12-21 weeks.  Call 690-129-3604 to make an appointment.

You will need to follow up with the neurosurgeon, Dr.
Carpenter in 06-22 weeks regarding the bleed in your head.
Inform the office that you may  need a repeat CT scan before
this.  Call 37-765-8737 to make an appointment.

You will need to follow up with the spinal doctors regarding
your neck fracture in 2 weeks.  Call 379-677-3160



Completed by:2011-09-08
